core_meaning: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる means 'to show' — to deliberately let someone see something, or to display/demonstrate something to another person. Unlike <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える (to be visible), <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる always implies an intentional agent doing the showing.

sentences.jp: ねえ、<ruby>新<rt>あたら</rt></ruby>しいスマホ<ruby>買<rt>か</rt></ruby>ったんだ。<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてよ！
sentences.translation: Hey, you bought a new phone? Let me see it!
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてよ is a very natural casual request form, softer than <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せろ but still direct.

sentences.jp: <ruby>先生<rt>せんせい</rt></ruby>に<ruby>宿題<rt>しゅくだい</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せた。
sentences.translation: I showed my homework to the teacher.
sentences.notes: The basic transitive pattern: [person に] [thing を] <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>はステージで<ruby>素晴<rt>すば</rt></ruby>らしいパフォーマンスを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せた。
sentences.translation: She put on a wonderful performance on stage.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる can mean 'to put on a display of' or 'to demonstrate' an ability or quality, not just show a physical object.

sentences.jp: パスポートをご<ruby>提示<rt>ていじ</rt></ruby>ください、とも<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>えますが、パスポートを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてください、でも<ruby>十分<rt>じゅうぶん</rt></ruby>です。
sentences.translation: You can also say 'please present your passport,' but 'please show your passport' is perfectly fine too.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてください is polite and usable even in semi-formal situations like customs or offices.

sentences.jp: <ruby>絶対<rt>ぜったい</rt></ruby>に<ruby>成功<rt>せいこう</rt></ruby>してみせる！
sentences.translation: I'll absolutely show you (that) I can succeed!
sentences.notes: 〜てみせる is a set grammatical construction meaning 'I'll do X and prove it / show everyone'; it expresses strong determination.

dont_use: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる requires intent — don't use it when something is simply visible or comes into view by accident; use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える instead (e.g., '<ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える,' not '<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる'). Also avoid confusing <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる with <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る — <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる is transitive and needs an object being shown to someone, while <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る is about the viewer's own act of looking. Don't overuse ご<ruby>覧<rt>らん</rt></ruby>に<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる as a substitute; that extremely humble form is only for very formal or service contexts.

confused_with.contrast: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる is intentional ('to show someone'), while <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える is spontaneous ('to be visible / to appear'). Compare: <ruby>写真<rt>しゃしん</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せた (I showed them the photo — deliberate) vs. <ruby>遠<rt>とお</rt></ruby>くに<ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えた (a mountain was visible in the distance — no one 'showed' it). <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える also has a secondary meaning of 'to seem/look like,' as in <ruby>若<rt>わか</rt></ruby>く<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える ('you look young'), which <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる cannot do.

frequency: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる is very common in everyday spoken and written Japanese at all levels. It appears constantly in daily conversation, instruction, customer service, and narrative writing. The grammatical construction 〜てみせる is also common in books, manga, and drama for expressing determination.
anki_hint: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる = intentional showing (you choose to do it); remember 〜てみせる as 'I'll do it and prove it!' — a sign of strong resolve.
